name: Erskine Hawkins
description: American trumpeter and big band leader from Birmingham, Alabama (1914-1993)
abstract: Erskine Ramsay Hawkins (July 26, 1914 – November 11, 1993) was an American trumpeter and big band leader from Birmingham, Alabama, dubbed "The 20th Century Gabriel". He is best remembered for composing the jazz standard "Tuxedo Junction" (1939) with saxophonist and arranger Bill Johnson.

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Pittsburgh Courier Poll Winners Jazz Concert

quotes.text: Well, I played most of the big spots. I did a concert in Carnegie Hall. That was during the big band years which I, during the time when they had a paper called Pittsburgh Courier out at that time. That was one of the highlights. When I first went into, I was looking forward, I played the Apollo and all them now cause I loved that. But, I was looking forward to going downtown and playing the Paramount, the Strand and all that. And
quotes.caption: Hawkins recalls performing at Carnegie Hall during big band era

about_quotes.speaker: Bobby Johnson Jr.
about_quotes.evidence: I stayed with them twelve years... Erskine always had a trumpet soloist... I got a chance to record... I recorded the most trumpet solos with another trumpet player, another great trumpet player’s band
about_quotes.doc_title: Bobby Johnson Jr. interviewed by Monk Rowe, Ellenville, New York, June 23, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Steve Allen
about_quotes.evidence: I began to listen to Ellington records and Basie records and Erskine Hawkins and Fletcher Henderson and all the good jazz bands of that day.
about_quotes.doc_title: Steve Allen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Bryant
about_quotes.evidence: I did it and I played it with the orchestra... It was one of the dreams of my life, came true.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Bryant interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, March 22, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Buddy Tate
about_quotes.evidence: >> [INTERVIEWEE] Erskine Hawkins. It didn't catch on as much as Jacquet's did, but he did that and a cat asked me once, why did Texas players have the big sounds, most of them? So I told him once, the way I could answer, I said, 'Well, there's a big stage. You got to play loud to be heard.'

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee discusses Erskine Hawkins' role in the band and the recording of 'Tuxedo Junction' from a historical/observational perspective, referring to him as 'he' and describing his playing style, but does not claim a personal relationship, collaboration, or direct interaction.

about_quotes.speaker: Doc Cheatham
about_quotes.evidence: Cause they were more interested in bands like the Savoy Sultans, they stayed up there, because the people were jumping more to that type of band, than they were Benny Carter's band. So they liked a band like the Sultans and Erskine Hawkins, type of band came and stayed there.
